_about.title: Indian Founder Library
_about.purpose: Hand-curated, link-verified resources every young Indian founder needs. Each entry has been chosen because it appears in the WTF + BarberShop corpus, is operated by a guest on those shows, or is a canonical Indian-founder resource (gov.in registry, an SEBI-registered fund, or a long-standing operator-grade tool).
_about.rule: No entry without a working URL on a stable domain. No entry whose existence cannot be checked from a publicly-listed source. No invented founders, funds, or initiatives.

sections.title: Register the company
sections.subtitle: The exact statutory rails every Indian founder must clear in the first 90 days.

items.name: MCA · Company Incorporation (SPICe+)
items.description: Single web form (SPICe+) to incorporate a Pvt Ltd / OPC, get PAN, TAN, EPFO, ESIC, GST, and a bank-account opening slot in one filing.

items.name: Startup India · DPIIT Recognition
items.description: Free recognition that unlocks 80-IAC tax holiday, angel-tax exemption, and access to the Fund of Funds for Startups (FFS).

items.name: Udyam · MSME Registration
items.description: Free MSME (Micro/Small/Medium) registration. Required for priority-sector lending, GeM listing, and most state-level subsidies.

items.name: GST · Registration & e-Invoicing
items.description: Mandatory once turnover crosses ₹40 L (goods) / ₹20 L (services). Get this before opening a payment gateway — gateways will ask for it.

items.name: GeM · Government e-Marketplace
items.description: The procurement rail every CPSE, ministry, and state government buys through. Sellers with Udyam recognition get preference and an EMD waiver.

items.name: DPDP Act · Practitioner Brief
items.description: MeitY's official Digital Personal Data Protection Act page — current consent, breach-notification, and data-fiduciary obligations.

sections.title: Capital · Government rails
sections.subtitle: Non-dilutive Indian rupee capital that exists today, with eligibility windows you can actually meet.

items.name: Startup India Seed Fund Scheme (SISFS)
items.description: Up to ₹20 L grant (PoC) or ₹50 L convertible note via 200+ DPIIT-approved incubators. DPIIT recognition + ≤2 years old.

items.name: SIDBI Venture Capital · Fund of Funds for Startups
items.description: ₹10,000 Cr fund that backs SEBI-registered AIFs which then invest in DPIIT-recognised startups. Founders access it via the AIFs SIDBI has anchored.

items.name: PM Mudra Yojana (PMMY)
items.description: Loans up to ₹20 L (Tarun-Plus) for non-farm, non-corporate small businesses. Active rail through 30+ scheduled commercial banks; the default first-cheque for tier-2/3 founders without VC reach.

items.name: CGTMSE Credit Guarantee
items.description: Collateral-free credit guarantee up to ₹5 Cr for MSMEs. The mechanism behind 'collateral-free working capital' from most public-sector banks.

items.name: BIRAC · Biotech Ignition Grant (BIG)
items.description: Grants up to ₹50 L for early-stage biotech and life-sciences ventures. Twice-yearly call. The default starting line for any Indian deeptech / health founder.

items.name: MeitY · TIDE 2.0 / Genesis
items.description: MeitY-anchored grants and incubation support across 51 EIRs. Defaults sit at ₹4 L–₹25 L per startup over 12 months.

sections.title: Capital · Private funds
sections.subtitle: VCs, founder-led funds, and venture-debt platforms repeatedly named on WTF + BarberShop. SEBI-registered or named publicly by a podcast guest.

items.name: Rainmatter Capital
items.description: Zerodha's in-house long-horizon fund — climate, capital markets, health, and frontier consumer. Operated by Nikhil Kamath's team; cheque sizes ₹2 Cr–₹50 Cr.

items.name: WTFund
items.description: ₹25 L grant + 1-year program for founders under 25. Run by Nikhil Kamath; explicitly grant-first, no equity at the entry round.

items.name: Peak XV Partners
items.description: Formerly Sequoia India & SEA. Largest India-anchored multi-stage VC; runs Surge accelerator (~$3M) for first cheque.

items.name: Z47
items.description: Formerly Matrix Partners India. Seed-to-Series-B firm; long-time backer of Razorpay, Ola, Country Delight.

items.name: Accel India
items.description: Early-stage firm that wrote the first cheques into Flipkart, Freshworks, Swiggy, and BlackBuck. Atoms accelerator runs once a year.

items.name: Elevation Capital
items.description: Formerly SAIF Partners India. Series A specialist. Notable bets: Paytm, Swiggy, Meesho, Spinny.

items.name: Blume Ventures
items.description: Domestic seed-stage VC anchored in INR LP base. Seed cheques ₹4 Cr–₹15 Cr, very founder-friendly term sheets.

items.name: 3one4 Capital
items.description: Bengaluru-based early-stage fund (Pranav & Siddarth Pai). Focused on consumer, fintech, and SaaS; LP base includes domestic family offices.

items.name: Stellaris Venture Partners
items.description: Early-stage SaaS / consumer / fintech fund. Notable: Mamaearth, Whatfix, Slintel.

items.name: Lightspeed India
items.description: Series A/B fund. Bets: OYO, Udaan, Byju's, Yellow.ai. Co-leads with Peak XV / Accel on most large rounds.

items.name: Fireside Ventures
items.description: Pure-play consumer brand fund (Kanwal Singh). Backed boAt, Mamaearth, Yoga Bar, Anveshan.

items.name: Sauce.vc
items.description: Manu Chandra's seed-stage consumer fund. Cheque sizes ₹2 Cr–₹10 Cr; hands-on with brand and hiring.

items.name: Titan Capital
items.description: Kunal Bahl & Rohit Bansal's family office. Has angel-invested in 250+ Indian startups including Razorpay, Urban Company, Mamaearth.

items.name: 100X.VC
items.description: Sanjay Mehta's seed fund. iSAFE notes (₹1.25 Cr at ₹15 Cr cap) for pre-revenue founders. Two cohorts a year.

items.name: Antler India
items.description: Pre-team residency program — 6 months in Bengaluru, ₹40 L for ~10% post co-founder match. Starts with no idea required.

items.name: Alteria Capital
items.description: Largest Indian venture-debt platform. Tickets ₹5 Cr–₹100 Cr against ARR / inventory. Useful between Series A and B.

items.name: Trifecta Capital
items.description: Venture debt + growth equity. Has funded Cars24, BharatPe, Pharmeasy. Often pairs alongside Alteria for larger debt rounds.

items.name: Recur Club
items.description: Non-dilutive revenue-based financing for SaaS / subscription businesses. Trade ARR for cash within 48 hrs; ticket sizes ₹50 L–₹100 Cr.

sections.title: Operator stack
sections.subtitle: The default tooling layer underneath every Indian D2C / SaaS / consumer brand built on the shows.

items.name: Shopify · India
items.description: Default D2C storefront referenced across the WTF brand-build, beauty, and ecommerce episodes. Plug-and-play with Razorpay, Shiprocket, Meta, GA4.

items.name: Razorpay
items.description: Payments + payroll + capital. The default Indian payment gateway; 100+ payment modes including UPI, EMI, COD reconciliation.

items.name: Cashfree Payments
items.description: Bank-grade payment + payouts API. Common alternative to Razorpay; strong in marketplace + B2B payouts use cases.

items.name: Shiprocket
items.description: D2C shipping aggregator integrated with 17+ couriers. The shipping rail behind most ₹10 Cr–₹500 Cr Indian D2C brands.

items.name: Meesho · Supplier Panel
items.description: Zero-commission supplier dashboard. The fastest way to test value-tier demand in tier-2/tier-3 India for a new SKU.

items.name: Udaan
items.description: B2B distribution rail. Useful for FMCG / electronics / fashion brands trying to reach 1.6M kirana retailers without owning the field force.

items.name: Amazon Seller Central · India
items.description: Marketplace + advertising + FBA logistics. Use the Search Term reports as a free demand-mapping tool before committing inventory.

items.name: Flipkart Seller Hub
items.description: Tier-2/3 strength, especially in fashion and electronics. Sell-Through report exposes regional preference patterns Meta ads can't.

items.name: Meta for Business
items.description: Cheapest creative-testing rail in India — Ads Manager, Meta Pixel, Conversions API. The creative learnings feed back into the brand voice, not just the funnel.

items.name: Google Ads · India
items.description: Search + YouTube + Performance Max. Most accurate intent data for a category before you commit to a wedge.

items.name: Zoho · One
items.description: Indian-built operating-system bundle: CRM, books, mail, payroll, support. Cheapest viable stack for sub-50-person teams.

items.name: Notion
items.description: Working-doc layer for early teams. The hand-off OS that keeps async ops sustainable below 30 people.

items.name: Linear
items.description: Issue tracker for product / engineering teams. Fast keyboard-first UX; replaces Jira for tech-first companies.

items.name: Slack
items.description: Default async comms layer. Channels-not-DMs is the cultural hack you implement on day one.

items.name: Keka
items.description: Indian-grown HRMS used across 8,000+ Indian companies. Payroll + leave + PF + ESIC + appraisals in one stack.

sections.title: Books named on the shows
sections.subtitle: Books referenced by guests on WTF or BarberShop. Sourced from the dossiers and ingest-script's RESOURCE_SEEDS.

items.name: Principles · Ray Dalio
items.description: Operating system for decisions, incentives, and truth-seeking. Discussed at length in WTF Ep #2 (Ray Dalio).

items.name: The Psychology of Money · Morgan Housel
items.description: How behaviour, not IQ, shapes wealth. Frequently cited across WTF Finance and BarberShop wealth episodes.

items.name: Zero to One · Peter Thiel
items.description: Framework for monopolies and category creation. Ground-zero reading for the BarberShop S1 Aman Gupta and Asish Mohapatra arcs.

items.name: The Hard Thing About Hard Things · Ben Horowitz
items.description: Field guide for hard calls under pressure. Surfaces in Toshan Tamhane / Rohit Kapoor leadership conversations on BarberShop.

items.name: Poor Charlie's Almanack · Charlie Munger
items.description: Mental models for compounding builders. Stripe Press edition is now the canonical print. Cited by Nikhil in WTF wealth conversations.

items.name: Blue Ocean Strategy · Kim & Mauborgne
items.description: Category creation and uncontested-market positioning. The intellectual scaffolding behind the BarberShop Solara / baby-care theses.

items.name: Sapiens · Yuval Noah Harari
items.description: Long-range context for belief systems and institutions. Repeatedly recommended on WTF as a foundation read.

sections.title: Compliance & sectoral regulators
sections.subtitle: The named bodies a founder will eventually have to deal with. Knowing the URLs cuts weeks off the cycle.

items.name: SEBI · Securities and Exchange Board of India
items.description: Regulator for any AIF, PMS, mutual fund, or capital-markets product. Mandatory once you touch investor money or run a wealth product.

items.name: RBI · Reserve Bank of India
items.description: Regulates banks, NBFCs, payment systems, and account aggregators. PA / PG licences live here; RBI's payments-vision-doc is the ground truth.

items.name: IRDAI · Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority
items.description: Insurance regulator. Required for insurtech, embedded insurance, or motor / health-claims businesses.

items.name: CDSCO · Central Drugs Standard Control Organization
items.description: Drugs, devices, and cosmetics regulator. Indian baby-care / beauty / supplement brands all clear CDSCO before public launch.

items.name: FSSAI · Food Safety and Standards Authority of India
items.description: Required for any consumable — F&B, supplements, beverages. License classes (Basic / State / Central) tied to turnover.

items.name: BIS · Bureau of Indian Standards
items.description: Mandatory ISI / Compulsory Registration Scheme (CRS) for electronics, helmets, and 700+ product categories. Skip it and Customs holds shipments.

items.name: Trade Marks Registry · IP India
items.description: Trademark filing for brand mark + logo. Apply through the official portal — saves the ₹15K agent markup.

sections.title: Founder communities
sections.subtitle: Where the operators on these shows actually go when they need help.

items.name: NASSCOM
items.description: Indian IT industry body — flagship NASSCOM Product Conclave; 10,000 Startups program; CoE labs for AI / IoT / cyber.

items.name: TiE · Global Indian-Entrepreneur Network
items.description: 63-chapter global network with strong Bengaluru / Delhi / Mumbai chapters. Best for first-time founder mentorship + angel intros.

items.name: iSPIRT Foundation
items.description: The think tank behind India Stack (Aadhaar, UPI, ONDC, DigiLocker). PlayBookHQ events for product founders are invitation-only but free.

items.name: Headstart Network
items.description: Indian volunteer-run network — Startup Saturdays in 30+ cities. Lowest-friction path to a first peer cohort.

items.name: ONDC · Open Network for Digital Commerce
items.description: DPIIT-backed open commerce protocol. Onboarding as a seller / buyer app is free; lowers acquisition cost vs. closed marketplaces.

items.name: Y Combinator · Apply
items.description: $500K standard deal (post-W24). Indian founders consistently in the top 5 country cohorts since 2018; the bar moved high but still applicable.

sections.title: Free learning paths
sections.subtitle: Operator-grade study material that costs nothing and doesn't require a subscription.

items.name: Varsity by Zerodha
items.description: Long-form modules on markets, options, derivatives, personal finance. Built by the same Zerodha team that hosts WTF.

items.name: Varsity · YouTube
items.description: Free on-air sessions with practising market operators. Companion stream to Varsity's text modules.

items.name: Stripe Atlas Guides
items.description: Long-form playbooks on equity, hiring, fundraising, and selling worldwide. The closest thing to an MBA you can read in a weekend.

items.name: First Round Review
items.description: Operator-written essays — hiring, GTM, founder mode. Indian founders use it to short-cut leadership lessons that take a decade to learn live.

items.name: a16z Future Library
items.description: Andreessen Horowitz's open writing library — venture / fintech / consumer / bio / crypto theses. Free, quotable, unusually well-edited.

items.name: Paul Graham Essays
items.description: PG essay archive. Required reading: 'Do Things That Don't Scale', 'How to Start a Startup', 'Startup = Growth'.
